old_claim.note: Border Customs Posts provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The scoped practice is an inscribed written law/accounting record that specifies tax portions, weights, fees, guarantors, and account rendering.

source_shape.source_locator: AIO RO 26 translates the 374/3 BC inscription, including provisions for tax portions, Piraeus transport, weighing, fees, guarantors, sale proceeds, and rendering accounts.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source presents a written inscribed law/accounting record for harbor-linked grain tax administration.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The prior edge made a customs checkpoint the mechanism; the source shows the scoped record itself depends first on written fiscal/accounting instructions.

ontology_before: Port Customs Ledgers depended on Border Customs Posts, implying physical checkpoint infrastructure was the direct mechanism for the records.
ontology_after: Port Tax Accounts is scoped to written harbor-linked tax/accounting records, so writing carries the direct record format while currency and public archives provide financial and publication context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: port_customs_ledgers now requires writing and no longer directly depends on border_customs_posts.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to physical border inspection stations rather than written harbor-linked tax and cargo accounts.
A source shows the earliest scoped port accounts were oral checkpoint practices with no written record component.
A separate customs_checkpoint_records node is added and should take the border_customs_posts dependency.
short_reason: The source-backed mechanism is a written tax/accounting record, not the border post itself.
